description: English Holly (Ilex aquifolium) is a common ornamental plant toxic to cats. It contains a complex of irritant saponins (including ilicin), methylxanthine-related compounds, and cyanogens concentrated primarily in the berries and leaves. The bright red berries are particularly attractive to cats and pose the greatest risk.
name: English Holly
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: ilicin and related saponins, methylxanthine-related compounds, trace cyanogens
Mechanism: saponins cause intense GI mucosal irritation; systemic absorption may affect the central nervous system
Symptoms: intense nausea, repeated vomiting, diarrhea, lethargy, excessive head shaking; severe ingestion may cause drooling, lip-smacking, CNS depression
Even 1–2 berries can produce significant symptoms in a cat
Keep all holly plants — fresh, dried, or decorative — out of reach; seek vet care if ingestion is suspected

symptoms.name: Vomiting, Diarrhea, and Nausea
symptoms.onset: Acute (Clinical signs typically appear shortly after ingestion,

symptoms.name: Abdominal Pain
symptoms.onset: Acute (Associated with the onset of vomiting and diarrhea).

symptoms.name: Drooling / Hypersalivation
symptoms.onset: Immediate to Acute (Upon chewing or swallowing).

symptoms.name: Lethargy, Depression, or Decreased Activity
symptoms.onset: Associated with the systemic malaise following ingestion.
